# ADR-260021: Evolution and Extensibility Baseline

## Workflow

- This ADR records accepted J1-J5 evolution and extensibility decisions.
- The decision is accepted because long-term adaptability depends on explicit growth boundaries and migration safeguards.

Status: Accepted
Date: 2026-04-17
Owners: Repository owner, product collaborator

## Context

The project has now established MVP scope, UX model, architecture, stack criteria, and delivery baselines.

To avoid future rework and rigidity, the product needs clear guidance on which post-MVP capabilities are anticipated, what must remain deferred in MVP, and how architecture should absorb change safely.

## Decision

- J1 accepted decision:
  - Anticipate post-MVP capabilities now through extension seams: multiple graph modes, saved queries/views, label governance/admin workflows, production auth/permissions, and collaboration tooling.
- J2 accepted decision:
  - MVP shall not be distorted by premature implementation of real-time collaboration engines, full admin suites, plugin runtime systems, multi-runtime frontend splits, or heavy analytics surfaces.
- J3 accepted decision:
  - Frontend shall use adapter-based mapping to remain resilient to evolution in atom metadata, bond semantics, nucleary depth, label taxonomy, and compatibility/error metadata.
- J4 accepted decision:
  - MVP remains single-user, while architecture preserves collaboration readiness via action identity/timestamps, persisted-vs-local state separation, and no hard single-editor assumptions.
- J5 accepted decision:
  - Migration resilience depends on strict boundaries: graph/auth/data wrappers, feature public APIs, centralized api-contract boundary, and contract tests at feature seams.

## Alternatives Considered

- Option A: Optimize only for MVP and revisit extensibility after release.
- Option B: Build full collaboration/admin/plugin systems in MVP.
- Option C: Keep MVP lean while codifying extension seams and migration safeguards.

## Consequences

- Positive:
  - Preserves agility for user-driven adaptations without overengineering MVP.
  - Reduces future replacement risk through boundary-based architecture.
  - Clarifies which growth investments are deliberate deferrals.
- Negative:
  - Requires ongoing governance discipline to enforce seams and boundaries.
  - Deferred advanced capabilities may be requested sooner than planned.
- Risks:
  - If adapters and contracts are not maintained, schema evolution may still leak into UI internals.

## Verification

- Architecture checks confirm wrapper and public-interface boundaries are used.
- Scope checks confirm deferred heavy capabilities remain outside MVP delivery.
- Data-flow checks confirm adapter layer isolates backend model evolution from UI features.
- Contract checks confirm seam tests protect migration-critical interfaces.

## Rollback

- Supersede this ADR if adaptation evidence shows new evolution priorities.
- Update linked requirements and backlog artifacts to the superseding decision.
